Brief Summary: Several stops in one neighborhood with staggered time preferences representing opening times.
Core Test: Tests arrival-time optimization — multiple stops in the same area open at staggered times. The engine should sequence visits to minimize waiting.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 4-6 stops clustered in one Manhattan neighborhood (e.g., Lower East Side). Give 3-4 stops TimePreference values at staggered morning times (9 AM, 10 AM, 11 AM, 12 PM). All Mandatory=true. Short durations (30-45 min each). The engine must order them to avoid arriving before opening.

Brief Summary: Two mandatory stops with a low-priority optional stop geographically between them.
Core Test: Tests "free stop" inclusion — a low-priority optional stop sits directly on the geographic path between two high-priority mandatory stops. Does the engine grab it?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 4-5 stops. Place 2 mandatory stops in neighborhoods that create a natural transit path (e.g., SoHo and Midtown). Place 1 optional stop (Mandatory=false) at a location directly on that path (e.g., Greenwich Village). Add 1-2 other stops elsewhere. The engine should recognize the "free" stop.

Brief Summary: Many stops, none mandatory, no time constraints. Total freedom for the engine.
Core Test: Tests self-organization — with zero mandatory stops and no time preferences, can the engine produce a coherent, geographically logical route on its own?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 7-10 stops across varied Manhattan neighborhoods. Set ALL stops to Mandatory=false and ALL TimePreference=null. Use a wide time window (10+ hrs). The engine must decide ordering, inclusion, and routing entirely on its own.

Brief Summary: Mix of non-negotiable timed stops and "preferred but flexible" timed stops that may conflict.
Core Test: Tests constraint hierarchy — the engine must always satisfy hard time constraints (tickets/reservations) and treat preferred times as flexible when conflicts arise.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 4-6 stops. Include 1-2 stops with hard Mandatory=true and specific TimePreference (e.g., museum ticket at 2 PM). Include 1-2 stops with Mandatory=false but with a TimePreference that is close to or conflicts with the hard window (e.g., preferred lunch at 1 PM nearby). Add flexible stops to fill gaps.

Brief Summary: 10+ stops in one neighborhood but only enough time for 4-5. Mix of mandatory and optional.
Core Test: Tests subset selection — more stops requested than can physically be visited. The engine must pick the highest-value subset and drop the rest.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 8-10 stops all in one Manhattan neighborhood (e.g., Chinatown or East Village). Set time window to only 3-4 hours. Make 2-3 stops Mandatory=true, rest optional. Durations of 30-60 min each. Mathematically impossible to visit all. Engine must select a subset.

Brief Summary: 3-4 stops with fixed times that force a north→south→north or zigzag path.
Core Test: Tests ordering under hard time constraints — fixed appointment times force a geographic sequence that may require backtracking or long transit legs.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 4-6 stops. At least 3 must be Mandatory=true with specific TimePreference values that force geographic zigzagging (e.g., Harlem at 11 AM, Financial District at 2 PM, Upper West Side at 5 PM). Add 1-2 optional stops without time preferences.

Brief Summary: One mandatory midday appointment. Other stops must be organized before and after it.
Core Test: Tests schedule construction around a fixed midpoint — one unmovable appointment in the middle of the day forces morning and afternoon segments to be built around it.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-7 stops. One stop must be Mandatory=true with a midday TimePreference (e.g., 1:00 PM) in a specific neighborhood. Place remaining stops (mix of mandatory/optional, no TimePreference) in neighborhoods both north and south of the anchor. Time window should span full day.

Brief Summary: All stops walkable, no subway or car allowed. Tests if the route stays geographically tight.
Core Test: Tests geographic efficiency without transit — walk-only restriction means the engine must keep stops tightly clustered or accept very long walks.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 4-6 stops. Set AllowedTransport to walking only. Stops should be in nearby but not identical neighborhoods (e.g., SoHo, West Village, Greenwich Village, East Village). Normal time window. The engine has no transit options and must optimize walking distance.

Brief Summary: Full day of stops but exit time is early afternoon, making it impossible to hit everything.
Core Test: Tests graceful degradation — a normal-looking trip with a suddenly shortened time window forces the engine to drop low-priority stops.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 6-8 stops spread across Manhattan. Set a short time window (e.g., 10 AM to 3 PM — only 5 hours). Mix mandatory and optional stops with durations totaling well over 5 hours. The engine must drop optional stops gracefully while preserving mandatory ones.

Brief Summary: Two time-locked stops with a 2-3 hour gap between them, plus several optional stops nearby.
Core Test: Tests gap-filling — given a large idle window between two fixed appointments, does the engine insert nearby optional stops to use dead time productively?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-7 stops. Include 2 mandatory stops with TimePreference values creating a 2-3 hour gap (e.g., Hell's Kitchen at 12 PM, Upper East Side at 4 PM). Add 3-5 optional stops (Mandatory=false, TimePreference=null) in neighborhoods between or near the anchored stops.

Brief Summary: Trip starts at 4-5 PM, ends at 11 PM-midnight. Stops are restaurants, shows, bars.
Core Test: Tests evening/nighttime routing — most scenarios are daytime. This tests dinner, shows, nightlife sequencing and late-hour transit availability.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with EntryTime around 4:00-5:00 PM and ExitTime around 11:00 PM-12:00 AM. Include 4-6 stops appropriate for evening: a dinner spot, a Broadway show or live music venue, a bar or lounge, and a park or landmark for golden hour. Mix neighborhoods.

Brief Summary: Lunch and dinner reservations with non-food stops in between.
Core Test: Tests activity sequencing logic — does the engine place meals at sensible times and avoid stacking food stops consecutively?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-7 stops. Include 2 food/restaurant stops with TimePreference at logical meal times (e.g., noon and 7 PM). Remaining stops should be non-food activities (museums, parks, shopping). The engine should sequence non-food activities between meals, not stack all food together.

Brief Summary: 5-6 stops all within one small neighborhood. Should be a simple, clean walking route.
Core Test: Tests tight-cluster baseline — all stops walkable within one neighborhood. If the engine introduces unnecessary transit or backtracking here, something is fundamentally broken.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-6 stops ALL within one Manhattan neighborhood (e.g., all in SoHo or all in East Village). All walkable distance from each other. No time preferences. Mix of mandatory and optional. Normal time window. This is a baseline sanity test — the route should be a clean walking loop.

Brief Summary: Start uptown, all stops ordered geographically south. No time anchors to complicate things.
Core Test: Tests linear path discovery — stops descend from uptown to downtown. The obvious optimal route is a straight line south. Does the engine find it?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest starting in upper Manhattan (e.g., Harlem or UWS). Place 5-7 stops that descend geographically southward through Midtown to downtown (e.g., Harlem → UWS → Midtown → Chelsea → West Village → Financial District). All Mandatory=true, no TimePreference. The optimal route is a linear sweep south.

Brief Summary: A tight cluster of 4-5 nearby stops plus one mandatory stop far away.
Core Test: Tests detour cost/benefit — does the engine handle one geographically distant stop among a tight cluster intelligently (include it, drop it, or sequence it efficiently)?
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-6 stops. 4-5 stops should be clustered in one Manhattan neighborhood (e.g., all in East Village). Add 1 mandatory stop in a distant neighborhood (e.g., Inwood or Washington Heights). Time window is normal (8-10 hrs). Test how the engine handles the long detour.

Brief Summary: 8-10 mandatory time-locked stops in a 5-6 hour window. Mathematically impossible to complete.
Core Test: Tests systemic overload and failure communication — every stop is mandatory with a time preference in a window too short to satisfy all of them. Different from Impossible Day (which is 2 conflicting stops); this is total overload.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 8-10 stops. ALL must be Mandatory=true with specific TimePreference values spread across a short 5-6 hour window. Stops spread geographically across Manhattan. Durations of 30-60 min each. The math does not work — total duration + travel time far exceeds the window. Tests engine failure handling.

Brief Summary: Start and end at the same location (e.g., a hotel). Stops spread across Manhattan.
Core Test: Tests return-leg awareness — the engine must account for travel time back to the starting/ending point, not just optimize the forward path.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-7 stops spread across Manhattan. Set StartLocation and EndLocation to the same place (e.g., a Midtown hotel). Include a firm ExitTime. The engine must leave enough time to return to start. Mix mandatory and optional stops.

Brief Summary: Stops spread across Manhattan but subway is disabled. Tests car vs. walk decisions.
Core Test: Tests transit mode flexibility — no subway forces car+walk combinations across longer distances, changing cost/time tradeoffs.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 5-7 stops spread across Manhattan (e.g., mix of downtown, midtown, uptown). Set AllowedTransport to car and walking only (no subway). Normal time window. The engine must decide when walking is sufficient vs. when to use a car for longer legs.

Brief Summary: Two groups of 3 stops each in distant neighborhoods with no stops in between.
Core Test: Tests cluster-travel-cluster efficiency — two tight geographic clusters with nothing in between. The engine should complete one cluster fully before traveling to the other.
LLM Generator Prompt: Generate an ItineraryRequest with 6 stops. 3 stops clustered in one Manhattan neighborhood (e.g., Harlem) and 3 clustered in a distant one (e.g., Financial District). No stops in between. No TimePreference on any. All Mandatory=true. The engine should do all of Cluster A, then travel, then do all of Cluster B — not interleave them.
